coredata.dc:title: ROS generation/scavenging modulation of carbon dots as phototherapeutic candidates and peroxidase mimetics to integrate with polydopamine nanoparticles/GOx towards cooperative cancer therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Getachew, Girum
Korupalli, Chiranjeevi
Rasal, Akash S.
Chang, Jia-Yaw
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging or generation ability of the carbon dots (CDs) was regulated by incorporating with heteroatoms (Cu and Cl ions). The pristine CDs were found to be powerful anti-oxidants to scavenge ROS, with half-maximal inhibitory concentrations (IC50) of •O2 − and •OH radicals estimated to be 6.89 and 6.12 μg/mL, respectively, whereas Cu and Cl co-doped CDs (CuCl-CDs) possessed not only ROS generation ability upon laser irradiation for photodynamic therapy (PDT), but also peroxidase-mimic activity that generates oxidative •OH from hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) for chemodynamic therapy (CDT). Moreover, the colorimetric assay, 1O2 emission peak, and ESR results supported the efficient production of •O2 −, •OH, and 1O2 radicals. Furthermore, CuCl-CDs with ROS-generating abilities and peroxidase-mimetic properties were successfully integrated with polydopamine (PDA) and glucose oxidase (GOx) to fabricate multifunctional GOx/CuCl-CD@PDA-PEG (GCP) nanocomposites with hydrodynamic sizes of 135.5 nm. These novel GCP nanocomposites possessed satisfactory photothermal conversion efficacies (η = 24.4%) and gave a high yield of ROS via the combination of H2O2 and laser irradiation. Moreover, the presence of GOx in GCP nanocomposites enables these compounds to decrease the intracellular glucose levels for starvation therapy and the enzymatic cascade activity for enhanced ROS-mediated therapy. In vitro studies and confirmed that these GCP nanocomposites displayed good biocompatibility with concentrations from 100 to 1000 ppm, but induced 90% reduction in B16F1 cell viability at 200 ppm via the cooperative effects of CDT, phototherapeutic effect, and starvation therapy.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: To assess the cellular uptake and intracellular localization of GCP nanocomposites, B16F1 were chosen as model cells using confocal laser scanning microscopy. Fig. 7h shows confocal microscopic images of B16F1 cancer cells stained with DAPI and treated with GCP nanocomposites at different concentrations. The images clearly show that the blue fluorescence from DAPI is the location of cell nuclei, while the intense red fluorescence signal of GCP nanocomposites was dispersed throughout the cytoplasm and nucleus of B16F1 cells. In addition, more intracellular fluorescence intensities were observed with increasing GCP nanocomposite doses. The mean fluorescence intensity of a single cell calculated from Fig. S7 also exhibited a linear correlation between the fluorescence intensities and the concentration of GCP nanocomposites. According to the above results, the cellular uptake efficiency of GCP nanocomposites is dose-dependent, which supports that the in vitro cell killing efficacy of PDT and PTT is dependent on the concentration of GCP nanocomposites (Fig. 7d–f).The authors would like to acknowledge financial supports from Ministry of Science and Technology of the Republic of China (Contract No. MOST 108-2218-E-011-017-MY3 and 110-2113-M-011-003). This work was also financially supported by Taiwan Building Technology Center from The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education in Taiwan. Thanks to Ms. C.-Y. Chien of Ministry of Science and Technology (National Taiwan University) for assistance with TEM experiments.

The authors would like to acknowledge financial supports from Ministry of Science and Technology of the Republic of China (Contract No. MOST 108-2218-E-011-017-MY3 and 110-2113-M-011-003 ). This work was also financially supported by Taiwan Building Technology Center from The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education in Taiwan . Thanks to Ms. C.-Y. Chien of Ministry of Science and Technology (National Taiwan University) for assistance with TEM experiments.

head.ce:title: ROS generation/scavenging modulation of carbon dots as phototherapeutic candidates and peroxidase mimetics to integrate with polydopamine nanoparticles/GOx towards cooperative cancer therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging or generation ability of the carbon dots (CDs) was regulated by incorporating with heteroatoms (Cu and Cl ions). The pristine CDs were found to be powerful anti-oxidants to scavenge ROS, with half-maximal inhibitory concentrations (IC50) of •O2 − and •OH radicals estimated to be 6.89 and 6.12 μg/mL, respectively, whereas Cu and Cl co-doped CDs (CuCl-CDs) possessed not only ROS generation ability upon laser irradiation for photodynamic therapy (PDT), but also peroxidase-mimic activity that generates oxidative •OH from hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) for chemodynamic therapy (CDT). Moreover, the colorimetric assay, 1O2 emission peak, and ESR results supported the efficient production of •O2 −, •OH, and 1O2 radicals. Furthermore, CuCl-CDs with ROS-generating abilities and peroxidase-mimetic properties were successfully integrated with polydopamine (PDA) and glucose oxidase (GOx) to fabricate multifunctional GOx/CuCl-CD@PDA-PEG (GCP) nanocomposites with hydrodynamic sizes of 135.5 nm. These novel GCP nanocomposites possessed satisfactory photothermal conversion efficacies (η = 24.4%) and gave a high yield of ROS via the combination of H2O2 and laser irradiation. Moreover, the presence of GOx in GCP nanocomposites enables these compounds to decrease the intracellular glucose levels for starvation therapy and the enzymatic cascade activity for enhanced ROS-mediated therapy. In vitro studies and confirmed that these GCP nanocomposites displayed good biocompatibility with concentrations from 100 to 1000 ppm, but induced 90% reduction in B16F1 cell viability at 200 ppm via the cooperative effects of CDT, phototherapeutic effect, and starvation therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are emerging as versatile fluorescent nanomaterials owing to their excellent properties, including high aqueous solubility, robust chemical inertness, easy modification, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity. According to previous studies, CDs have been widely used in a variety of applications, including bioimaging, photodetection, sensing, and cancer cell treatment [1–6]. Cancer cells possess high reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels compared to that of normal cells and maintain redox homeostasis by activating antioxidant pathways, and thus more susceptible to oxidative stress caused cell death [7–9]. Thus, generating ROS including superoxide radicals (•O2 −), alkoxyl radicals (ROO•), nitrosyl radical (NOO•), hydroxyl radical (•OH−) or singlet oxygen (1O2) by means of exogenous stimuli or agents, and thereby induce oxidative stress in cancer cells is a promising approach to abolish tumor cells efficiently [10,11]. For instance, photosensitizer-delivered nanosystems can respond to exogenous light irradiations to enable controllable ROS generation for photodynamic therapy (PDT); sonosensitizers-delivered nanosystems delivered nanoplatforms can be activated by ultrasound to enable efficient sonocatalytic ROS generation for sonodynamic therapy (SDT); inorganic nanozymes with peroxidase-like activities can convert endogenous biological H2O2 into highly cytotoxic •OH for chemodynamic therapy (CDT); ionizing radiation initiates aberrant physicochemical alterations in cancer cells, such as water ionization and consequent ROS generation for radiation therapy (RT) [9,12]. PDT is a non-invasive and mild medical approach for cancer treatment. PDT is a light-triggered therapy in which a combination of chemical photosensitizer (PS) and light irradiation transfers the energy of photons to molecular oxygen and generates cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS), including singlet oxygen (1O2), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and superoxide anions (•O2 −), to selectively damage tumor cell [13–17]. Unfortunately, low oxygen levels in the tumor microenvironment (TME) limit the amount of ROS generation. Another ROS-based cancer treatment strategy is chemodynamic therapy (CDT), which relies on a Fenton-like reaction that can produce cytotoxic •OH by catalysis of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) using Fenton ion [17,18]. Recently, peroxidase-mimic nanoenzymes have been demonstrated to trigger the conversion of H2O2 into highly reactive •OH in CDT [19–22]. However, the amount of H2O2 in cancer cells is insufficient to efficiently disproportionate H2O2 into •OH [10]. Furthermore, the maximized performance of the Fenton reaction occurred under a strong acidic environment (pH = 4.0) [23], which is lower than the mild acid condition (pH = 6.2–7.0) of TME. Therefore, combining PDT and CDT in one nanomaterial is important for maximizing ROS generation levels and improving the clinical application of ROS-mediated therapy [21,22].

According to the previous literature, we know that CDs can generate ROS upon light irradiation [4,5,24,25]. The possible mechanisms of ROS generation properties of CDs have been reported: energy transfer from the singlet state of CDs to its triplet state through intersystem crossing; subsequently, the long-lived triplet state of CDs interacts directly with ground-state oxygen (3O2), generating highly cytotoxic ROS. This distinctive feature enables CDs to serve as photosensitizers to eliminate cancer cells upon photoexcitation. Various polymer precursors, such as polythiophene derivate [4,6,24] and hyaluronic acid [25], have already been proven to serve as carbon sources in the hydrothermal synthesis of ROS-generating CDs. Jia et al. used manganese (II) phthalocyanine as a precursor to prepare Mn-CD with an excellent capability to generate 1O2 [2]. Wen and co-workers developed a solvothermal reaction to prepare hydrophobic CDs with photodynamic effects using pheophytin as a carbon source in N,N-dimethylformamide [26]. Additionally, some reports have demonstrated that nitrogen (N), chloride (Cl), copper (Cu), sulfur (S), fluorine (F), phosphorus (P), and their coalescence doping affected the photodynamic activity of CDs [27–32]. Moreover, CDs have been reported to exhibit peroxidase-mimic activity to generate •OH radicals [33–36]. Nevertheless, contradictory results have been obtained demonstrating that CDs possess intrinsic anti-oxidative properties and exhibit scavenging activity towards ROS, possibly due to several factors, including adduct formation, electron transfer process, and hydrogen donation [37]. For instance, Miao and his colleagues [38] reported rhodium nanoenzymes with superior radical scavenging activity which was reasoned to enhanced electron transfer kinetics and hydrogen donation. Recently, Li et al. [39] and James et al. [40] revealed the antioxidative properties of Se-doped CQD, and graphene QD respectively. According to their findings, the as-prepared nanomaterials have the potential to stabilize radical species via adduct formation, hydrogen ion donation, and mostly through electron transfer mechanisms. This means CDs themselves diminish the amount of generated ROS and weaken the efficacy of PDT or CDT. Therefore, the integration of photodynamic and chemodynamic ability in CDs is required to facilitate their potential bioapplications in ROS-mediated therapy.

Moreover, to maximize the therapeutic performance of ROS-based treatment strategies, PDT/CDT can be combined with various therapeutic strategies, such as starvation therapy and photothermal therapy (PTT), to offer new perspectives for the treatment of solid tumors. Cancer starvation therapy, which involves the shutdown of essential nutrients for cell survival and proliferation, is a promising therapeutic strategy for cancer treatment. The Warburg effect has revealed that 50% of cellular energy is generated from the glucose-related metabolic pathway. Therefore, blocking the glucose supply using glucose oxidase (GOx) enzyme, which oxidizes glucose into gluconic acid and H2O2 in the presence of O2, has become a research focus in recent times [41–45]. PTT involves conversion of light energy to sublethal cell hyperthermia, which is known to relieve tumor tissue hypoxia to some extent through increased blood flow into tumor [46–48].

In this study, CDs with ROS scavenging or generation abilities were synthesized using p-phenyldiamine (PPD) as a carbon source and Cu/Cl as a substitutional heteroatom. Pristine CDs showed antioxidant characteristics, whereas the incorporation of Cu and Cl ions inside the CDs (CuCl-CDs) enabled ROS generation capability, as shown in Scheme 1 a. Specifically, heteroatom incorporation endows CuCl-CDs with photodynamic effects upon laser irradiation for PDT and peroxidase-mimic activity that generates oxidative •OH from H2O2 for CDT. To further enhance the ROS-based treatment efficiency of CuCl-CDs, we also developed multifunctional GOx/Cu-CD@PDA-PEG (GCP) nanocomposites comprising polydopamine (PDA), CuCl-CDs, and glucose oxidase (GOx) to improve the therapeutic efficiency against cancer cells through the cooperative effect between PDT/CDT, glucose-mediated starvation therapy, and PTT. In this nanoplatform, PDA, a well-known natural biopolymer, could serve as a PTT agent with high photothermal conversion efficiency and act as a good carrier because of its outstanding biocompatibility and good chemical reactivity towards amine or thiol groups via Michael addition or Schiff base formation. The natural enzyme GOx functions as an effective catalytic substrate for glucose depletion and triggers a cascade of catalytic reactions with peroxidase-mimic activity of CuCl-CDs for •OH generation. Overall, by using these multifunctional nanocomposites, we could achieve an augmentative synergistic efficiency of starvation therapy, phototherapy, and CDT, which effectively suppressed cancer growth in a more efficient and safer manner.

P-phenyldiamine (PPD, >99%) was obtained from Acros organics. Cupric (II) chloride (CuCl2, 99.99%), dopamine (98%), O-(2-Aminoethyl)-O-(2-carboxyethyl) polyethylene glycol hydrochloride (PEG-NH2, Mw 3000), D-(+)-Glucose (≥99.5%), triethylamine (Et3N, ≥99%), hydroxylamine solution (NH2OH, 50%w/w), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA, 99%), 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, 98%), nitro blue tetrazolium (NBT, 98%), nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH, ≥97%), methylphenazonium methosulphate (PMS, ≥90%), 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP, ≥99%), 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO, ≥97%), dulbecco's modified eagle's medium high glucose (DMEM), minimum essential medium eagle (MEME), 2,7-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA, ≥97%), 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI, 98%), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3, 99.5–100.5%) and sodium pyruvate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. WST-1 (4-[3-(4-Iodophenyl)-2-[4-nitrophenyl]-2H-5-tetrazolio]-1,3-benzene disulfonate) ordered from Roche Applied Science. GOx from Aspergillus niger GOD (10 KU). Ammonia solution (NH4OH, 28–30%) was procured from SHOWA. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.5–38.0%), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), 1,4-diphenyl-2,3-benzofuran (DPBF, 99.99%) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from J.T.Baker. Iron (III) chloride (FeCl3, 98%) and trichloroacetic acid (TCA, 99%) were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%) was obtained from Honeywell Fluka™. Titanium (IV) sulfate (Ti(SO4)2, 24%) was purchased from Kanto Chemical CO. All chemicals were analytical grade and used directly without further purification.

To synthesize CuCl-CDs, 0.56 mmol PPD and 0.04 mmol CuCl2 were mixed in a microwave tube with 15 mL de-ionized (DI) water and sonicated for 5 min before being placed in a microwave reactor at 200 °C for 30 min (model Monowave 300). Subsequently, the resultant solution was cooled to room temperature before being purified by centrifugation at 2000 rpm for 1 h, the supernatant was collected and filtered through 0.25 μm pore size filter paper before being freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C. The same approach was used to prepare the pristine CDs, but without the addition of CuCl2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PDA and PDA-PEG nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The PDA NPs were synthesized in a Tris buffer solution (water-ethanol solvent system). Briefly, of 0.75 mL of NH4OH solution, 22.5 mL of deionized (DI) water, and 10 mL of ethanol were stirred together and 0.02 mmol of dopamine solution (50 mg/mL in DI water) was added to the mixture and agitated at 800 rpm at room temperature for 24 h [49]. After the completion of this reaction, the PDA nanoparticles (PDA NPs) were collected via centrifugation of the resulting brown-grey solution, rinsed with DI water to remove any ethanol residues, and dried in the oven at 40 °C for 2 h. For use in the experiment, the dried PDA NPs were dispersed in DI water using sonication.

To prepare surface-modified PDA-PEG NPs, 15 mg of PDA NPs were resuspended in 5 mL of aqueous solution under sonication, and 3 mL of PEG-NH2 (10 mg/mL) solution was injected into it and agitated at 800 rpm for 24 h. To remove the free PEG-NH2, the solution was dialyzed (50 K MWCO) against DI water for 2 d, freeze-dried to obtain PDA-PEG NP powder, and then dissolved in DI water as indicated.

By assuming the formation of covalent linkage, previously synthesized 2 mL of CuCl-CDs (5 mg/mL) and 2 mg of GOx were mixed into 5 mL of PDA-PEG NPs solution (5 mg/mL) and reacted for 12 h [50,51]. After completion of this reaction, the resulting solution was dialyzed (100 K MWCO) against DI water for 2 d to eliminate free GOx. The dialysate was then freeze-dried to obtain GCP nanocomposites in a powdered form.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS scavenging assay of CDs and CuCl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
By adapting the earlier studies [37], the superoxide radical scavenging test was carried out as follows: 0.2 mL of phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH = 6) and 0.1 mL of xanthine oxidase system (1 mM NADH and 15 μM PMS) were mixed in order to initiate the generation of •O2 − radicals. Then, 0.2 mL of NBT (50 μM) was added into the above mixture, resulting in the •O2 − radical-mediated conversion of NBT into blue formazan with a characteristic absorption at 560 nm. Next, 0.1 mL of various doses of CDs and CuCl-CD solution (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 14 μg/mL) were incubated with the previous mixture for 10 min. Then, the reduction in NBT absorbance was measured using UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy at 560 nm. The following equation was used to compute the inhibition value:
Inhibition value = (A1 - A2) /A1 × 100%
The absorbance intensities of the solution combination, with and without the sample, are shown as A1 and A2, respectively.

The abilities of CDs and CuCl-CDs to scavenge hydroxyl (•OH) radicals were investigated using a TMB solution (10 mM) prepared in DMSO. In the presence of 100 mM H2O2 solution, FeSO4·7H2O (10 mM) reagent was used to catalyze the generation of the •OH radical, followed by the addition of 50 μL of TMB. Subsequently, different concentrations of CDs and CuCl-CD solutions (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 14 μg/mL) were mixed with a solution containing •OH radicals. The absorbance spectra were measured at 652 nm. The above equation was used to calculate the inhibition efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodynamic activities of the GCP nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The ROS generating capacities of the GCP nanocomposite were evaluated by the DPBF assay [52]. In short, 20 μL of the DPBF probe solution (1.5 × 10−3 M) was mixed with 0.4 mL of GCP nanocomposites (100 μg/mL) and the mixture was irradiated at 1.12 W/cm2 using a 532 nm laser. Every 5 min, the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of DPBF was measured using a HORIBA Duetta spectrophotometer at room temperature. Subsequently, each fluorescence emission at 460 nm was compared to that of the control group, which was measured at 0 min.

For electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) studies, TEMP (100 mM in DMSO) and DMPO (100 mM in H2O and DMSO) were employed as spin traps for 1O2 and •O2 − (also •OH), respectively. In short, 100 μL of spin traps were combined with 0.9 mL CuCl-CDs in the same solvent system. Then, the mixture was irradiated with a 532 laser for 10 min and the EPR spectra were then collected. Note that 50 μL of H2O2 (100 mM) solution was introduced to examine the chemodynamic effect of CuCl-CDs.

To further investigate the detection of 1O2, the emission spectra at 1270 nm were measured. After saturating 3 mL of CuCl-CDs (100 μg/mL in D2O) with oxygen gas for 10 min, the solution was irradiated with a 532 nm laser at a 1.12 W/cm2 current supply. The fluorescence emission spectra were then obtained in the 1150–1350 nm wavelength range (λexcitation = 532 nm).

Following a previous report [53], the formation of •O2 − radicals was validated by the NBT assay. In a quartz cuvette, 200 μL of NBT (100 mM in water) was combined with 400 μL of CuCl-CDs (100 μg/mL) and irradiated for 5 min with a 532 nm laser (1.12 W/cm2). UV–Vis spectroscopy was used to monitor the absorbance of the solution at 260 nm.

GCP nanocomposites (1 mL) at various concentrations (0, 50, 75, and 100 μg/mL) were irradiated with an 808 nm laser (Hi-Tech Optoelectronics Co. Ltd. Beijing, China) and 2.0 W/cm2 power density to measure the photothermal performance of the nanocomposites and the solution temperatures recorded every 30 s for 20 min (10 min for each laser on and off). In another study, a GCP nanocomposite of 100 μg/mL was irradiated for the same period under an 808 laser at different laser power densities (1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 W/cm2). Roper's method [54] was used to determine the photothermal conversion efficiency of the GCP nanocomposite by monitoring the temperature change of the aqueous suspensions as a function of time (t) for 600 s under continuous irradiation with an 808 nm laser (2.0 W/cm2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: GOx-like enzymatic activity of GCP nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the GOx-like enzymatic activity, various concentrations of glucose (0, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 ppm) were produced and mixed with GCP nanocomposites or free GOx solution, followed by measurement of H2O2 and gluconic acid concentrations in the solution.

The concentration of gluconic acid was tested by adapting the previous procedure [55,56]. To begin, three reagent solutions were prepared: solution 1 (5 mM EDTA and 0.15 mM Et3N), solution 2 (3 mM NH2OH), and solution 3 (0.25 M CCl3COOH, 1 M HCl, and 0.1 M FeCl3). Separately, 10 μL of glucose solution (based on the aforementioned concentration) and 25 μL of GOx (100 ppm) or GCP (100 ppm) were added to 105 μL of PBS (pH = 5.5) and reacted for 6 h. Then, 125 μL of solution 1 and 12.50 μL of solution 2 were added to the reaction solution and allowed to react for 30 min before adding 62.5 μL of solution 3. Finally, a Biotech instrument was used to determine the absorbance at 505 nm. Because the formation of gluconic acid affects the pH of the solution, an advanced pH meter was used to determine the pH of the solution.

To detect H2O2, 24% TiSO4 was first prepared in 50 mL DI water containing 8.33 mL H2SO4. 2.1 mL of PBS, 0.2 mL glucose solution (100 mM), and 0.2 mL GCP nanocomposite (100 ppm) or GOx (100 ppm) were mixed in separate tubes. The absorbance at 405 nm was measured at 30 min intervals after 100 μL of glucose-containing solution was mixed with 200 μL of TiSO4.

The peroxidase activity of the GCP nanocomposites was evaluated by using a typical TMB assay and H2O2 as a substrate. GCP nanocomposites (100 μL, 15–160 ppm) and 40 μL of H2O2 (5–100 mM) were reacted in 150 μL PBS (at pH = 5.5) to generate •OH radicals, followed by addition of 30 μL of TMB solution (25 mM in DMSO) and the absorbance at 652 nm was measured using UV–Vis spectroscopy. During this process, changes in the color of the solution were observed.

For the kinetics experiment, 150 μL of PBS (pH = 5.5) was coupled with 40 μL of H2O2, 50 μL of GCP, and 25 μL of 25 mM TMB. The absorption intensity was measured at 652 nm. The same approach was used in another experiment, in which only glucose was utilized as the substrate instead of H2O2. The process was carried out with various concentrations of GCP, H2O2, and glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility of GCP nanocomposite in vitro studies
ce:section.ce:para: The WST-1 assay was used to analyze in vitro cell investigations. Cancer cells, such as human cervical (HeLa) cells, were cultivated at 37 °C and 5% CO2 in EMEM medium comprising 10% FBS, 1% nonessential amino acids, 1% sodium pyruvate, 1% l-glutamine, and 1% antibiotic-antimycotic component. In another set, mouse melanoma cancer (B16F1) and human liver carcinoma (HepG2) cells were cultured in DMEM medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1% nonessential amino acids, 1% sodium pyruvate, and 1% antibiotic component at 37 °C in an incubator fixed with 5% CO2.

Subsequently, the cancer cells were seeded at ∼1 × 104 cells per well in a flat-bottomed 96-well polymer-coated plate and cultured for 24 h at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator. The medium was then removed, the cells were washed with PBS, and a new cell medium containing GCP nanocomposites at various concentrations (0, 100, 200, 300, 500, 700, and 1000 μg/mL) was added to the wells containing the cells. After incubation for varying durations of time (24, 48, and 72 h), 10 μL of WST-1 reagent was added to each well (based on the manufacturer's protocol) and the plate was incubated for another 2 h. The absorbance at 450 nm was measured to estimate cellular viability using a model 680 XR microplate reader (Bio-Rad). The relative cell viability (%) was obtained by comparing the treated cells to the untreated control cells, cell viability (%) =
A o
A c
Χ 100 %
where Ao and Ac represent the absorbance of the sample and the control at 450 nm, respectively. For each test, including the control measurements, the experiment was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell viability through PDT and PTT effect
ce:section.ce:para: Cancer cells (HeLa, B16F1, and HepG2) were seeded on a 96-well plate at ∼1.0 × 104 cells per well for 24 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2 supply. Then, 0.1 mL of cell medium containing various concentrations of GCP was added to each well and incubated for 24 h under the same conditions. Next, the cells were illuminated with a laser at 532 nm (1.12 W/cm2) for 5 min to obtain PDT, followed by laser 808 nm (2.0 W/cm2) for 10 min to obtain PTT. After 24 h of incubation, the cells were washed with PBS and 0.1 mL of the new medium was supplied to the cell, and subsequently, 10 μL of WST-1 was added. After 2 h, the absorbance at 450 nm was measured, and the cell viability was calculated as previously described. Both lasers were employed to achieve combined PTT and PDT effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell viability through starvation effect
ce:section.ce:para: B16F1 cancer cells at a density of around ∼1.0 × 104 were cultured in a 96-well plate for 24 h in a humidified atmosphere at 37 °C and 5% CO2. The cells were rinsed in PBS and supplied 0.1 mL of culture medium with varying doses of glucose (0, 100, and 200 μg/mL) for one day under the same conditions. The cells were then washed with PBS to eliminate free glucose before being given 100 μL of fresh media containing 10 μL of various concentrations of GCP or GOx. The cells were washed with PBS to eliminate non-internalized material after 24 h of incubation, and new culture media were added. Subsequently, 10 μL of WST-1 was added, and cell viability was determined at 450 nm using a microplate reader.

After incubation with GCP nanocomposites, the cells were irradiated with a 532 nm laser (1.12 W/cm2) for 5 min and an 808 nm laser (2.0 W/cm2) for 10 min. Finally, cellular viability was estimated by measuring the absorbance at 450 nm using a model 680 XR microplate reader.

B16F1 cancer cells were allowed to grow for 24 h on glass slides placed in wells of a 6 well plate for cellular ingestion tests. The wells were then filled with fresh culture medium containing various concentrations of GCP and incubated for another day. After the free GCP-containing medium was removed, the cells were rinsed with PBS. Finally, 2 mL of ethanol (75% purity) was poured into each well and fixed for 10 min before staining the cell structure with DAPI as directed by the manufacturer. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was used to examine the fluorescent images of the cells using TCS SP2 equipment (Leica, Germany).

For intercellular ROS detection, B16F1 cancer cells were cultured in a medium containing different concentrations of GCP nanocomposites for 6 h. Then, the medium was removed, washed with PBS twice to remove the non-internalized samples, and fresh medium containing 10 μL of DCFH-DA was added to each well. After 2 h, the cells were irradiated with 532 nm laser power (1.12 W/cm2) for 5 min. Subsequently, the oxidized green DCFH-DA complex was formed inside the cytoplasm wall of a cancer cell. In the case of CDT efficiency (to see the generation of •OH radicals), 100 μL of glucose substrate (200 ppm) was introduced to the well before the addition of the GCP sample. Fluorescence images of the cells were observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) using a TCS SP2 device (Leica, Germany).

The photophysical properties of as-synthesized nanocomposites were examined by using UV–Visible absorption spectrometry (Model: FP-6500; Manufacturer: JASCO CO. Ltd) and PL spectrometry (Model: Fluorolog-3; Manufacturer: HORIBA JobinYvon). The morphological analysis was investigated by employing Transmission electron microscopy (Model: Tecnai F20 G2 FETEM; Manufacturer: Philips, and Model: JEM-2100F; Manufacturer: JEOL). The chemical and elemental composition was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using Thermo VG Scientific electron spectroscopy with an achromatic Al Kα source (1486.6 eV) as the X-ray excitation source. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected on a Nicolet 5700 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo electron, USA); Raman spectroscopy An UniG2D (UniNano Tech, Taiwan) was used to measure Raman spectra with a 532 nm CW laser as the light source; the Zetasizer 2000 (MALVERN). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Model: Nano-ZS90; Manufacturer: MALVERN) was used to determine the nanoparticle size distribution. Electron spin resonance (EPR) spectra were recorded at 10 K using a Bruker Elexsys E−580 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Viable cell counts were carried out by using ELISA reader (Model: Power Wave XS; Manufacturer: BioTek). The fluorescence and cellular imaging was observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) under a TCS SP2 device (Leica, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs and CuCl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs with ROS scavenging ability and CuCl-CDs with ROS generation capability were synthesized via microwave-assisted pyrolysis. Compared to a conventional heating up strategy, the microwave-assisted pyrolysis approach can provide more homogeneous heating, low energy consumption, and shorter reaction times, which are favorable for uniform nucleation growth and are essential for the formation of monodisperse nanoparticles.

The photophysical properties of the pristine CDs and CuCl-CDs were investigated using UV–Vis and PL spectra. As shown in Fig. 1 a, both CDs and CuCl-CDs demonstrated strong absorption peaks at approximately 302 and 494 nm, respectively. The absorption peak at approximately 302 nm is due to the π–π * transition, caused by the CC and CO bond interactions in the sp2 orbital of the aromatic stack, whereas the strong absorption at 494 nm is due to the transition of n-π* energy states or the presence of lower energy states resulting from the surface state. Additionally, the PL spectra indicated that the as-synthesized CDs and CuCl-CDs emitted fluorescence in the range of around 600–650 nm upon excitation at 490 nm, and the Cu and Cl dopants did not cause noticeable changes in the emission position, but notably enhanced the emission intensity (Fig. 1a). Upon optimization of the PPD and CuCl2 precursors, the PL quantum yields of the as-prepared CDs and CuCl-CDs were 4.71% (Figs. 1b) and 8.35% (Fig. 1c), respectively. The emission intensity of the CuCl-CDs varied according to the excitation wavelength (Fig. 1d).

The morphologies of the as-synthesized pristine CDs and CuCl-CDs were analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The high-resolution TEM images indicated that both pristine CDs (Fig. 1e) and CuCl-CDs (Fig. 1f) displayed a spherical morphology with a narrow size distribution. As shown in Fig. 1g, the interplanar distance of CuCl-CDs in the high-resolution TEM was 0.29 nm, corresponding to the (200) lattice planes. The DLS measurements presented the average particle size of pristine CDs and CuCl-CDs are about 5.9 nm and 6.4 nm as illustrated in Fig. 1h and i, respectively.

According to previous reports, it has been suggested that CDs exhibit intrinsic anti-oxidative properties for ROS scavenging applications, which will impair the ROS generation ability of CDs [57–61]. Thus, if the ROS scavenging capability of pristine CDs can be reduced, we can enhance the ROS generation ability of CDs. Moreover, Cu and Cl ions serve as dual dopants in CuCl-CDs to facilitate the production of ROS. In the structure, Cu ions act as dopants to generate •OH radicals via Fenton-like reactions, while Cl ions can effectively accelerate intersystem crossing to enhance ROS generation due to the heavy-atom effect. The lower ROS scavenging capability of the CuCl-CDs relative to pristine CDs could be due to the disruption of long-range aromatic regions (sp2 carbon atoms) in the CD framework due to the presence of heteroatom incorporation. Generally, molecules containing conjugated CC chains exhibit free radical scavenging activities. Likewise, CDs are carbon nanomaterials, consisting of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb lattice and have been reported to possess radical scavenging activities through adduct formation or electron transfer mechanisms.

The incorporation of Cu and Cl heteroatoms into the framework of CDs can disrupt the intrinsic sp2 hybridized structure and substantially reduce the interaction between free radicals and CC bonds of pristine CDs, followed by lowering the free radical scavenging capability. Fig. S1a shows the Raman spectra with two main bands at approximately 1389 cm−1 (disordered D-band) and 1598 cm−1 (crystalline G-band). The relative intensities (ID/IG) of the D band to the G band were 0.89 and 0.96 for CDs and CuCl-CDs, respectively, which indicated that the CuCl-CDs have a higher disordered carbon structure than the pristine CDs because of the introduction of Cu and Cl heteroatoms in the framework of CDs.

Another interpretation could be the decline in functional groups with strong hydrogen donors in the CuCl-CDs. Most antioxidant compounds, such as phenols, diarylamines, and their derivatives, possess low bond dissociation enthalpy (BDE) of O–H (or N–H) bonds and act as hydrogen donors, thereby transferring protons from antioxidant compounds to free radicals. For example, the phenolic antioxidant (ArO–H) intercept reacts with •OH according to
(1) •OH + ArO–H → H–OH + ArO•

Moreover, as discussed in earlier studies, the abundant functional groups, such as amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups at the edge sites of CDs endow them with good ROS scavenging activity [37,60,62]. Similarly, the hydroxyl or amine functional groups on the surface of the obtained CDs without heteroatom incorporation may easily undergo dissociation and release hydrogen atoms with free radicals due to low BDE values. In contrast, the amount of these functional groups with hydrogen donors in CuCl-CDs could be reduced because of the coordination interactions between Cu2+ ions and carboxylate (or hydroxyl) groups at the edge sites of the CDs, leading to decreased radical scavenging activity of the CuCl-CDs.

These assumptions were validated by recording and comparing the FTIR and XPS spectra of the CDs and CuCl-CDs. The FTIR spectrum of the CuCl-CDs exhibited several characteristic peaks at 3695, 3329, 3201, 2922, 1690, 1526, and 1271 cm−1, assigned to O–H, N–H, C–H (sp2), C–H (sp3), CO, CN, and C–O, respectively. Moreover, there are two additional absorption bands at 1398 and 442 cm−1, which were attributed to Cu–O/Cu–N and C–Cl, respectively, in CuCl-CDs. For the CD samples, the absorption bands related to the stretching vibration of O–H (3500-3800 cm−1), N–H (3334 cm−1), sp2 C–H (3167 cm−1), sp3 C–H (2998 cm−1), CN (2335 cm−1), CO (1740 cm−1), CN (1519 cm−1), and C–O (1244 cm−1) are shown in Fig. S1b. Compared with CuCl-CDs, a broad and intense band in the range of 3500–3800 cm−1 suggests that the surface of pristine CDs has multiple hydroxyl groups, which greatly benefit the antioxidant activity similar to conventional polyphenol antioxidants. Additionally, CuCl-CDs exhibited a large reduction of O–H, but intense peaks at CO suggested that heteroatom incorporation affected the type of functional groups on the surface of CuCl-CDs during the hydrothermal carbonization process. The results are in good agreement with those obtained from the XPS spectra shown in Fig. 2 . The XPS survey spectrum of the CDs revealed the presence of 74.69% C, 6.81% N, and 11.5% O, whereas the XPS survey spectrum of CuCl-CDs contained 74.01% C, 5.01% N, 9.11% O, 2.03% Cu, and 0.84% Cl (Fig. 2a). In the Cu 2p spectrum (Fig. 2b), the peaks located at 933 and 956.7 eV correspond to Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2, respectively, revealing that Cu2+ was doped in CuCl-CDs. Moreover, the satellite lines of Cu2+ were observed at 964.7 eV and in the range of 938–948 eV. In Fig. 2c, the peaks of Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2 at 199.9 eV and 198 eV are attributed to Cl− in CuCl-CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2d) for the CuCl-CD sample can be deconvoluted into three Gaussian peaks at 285.1, 287.2, and 289.0 eV, which are assigned to C–C/CC, CO/CN, and C–O, respectively. The N 1s core-level spectrum of CuCl-CDs displays peaks of amino N (C–N) at 398.3 eV, pyridinic N (C–NC) at 400.1 eV, and graphitic N (N–C3) at 402.0 eV in Fig. 2e [60]. Furthermore, the O 1s region of CuCl-CDs in Fig. 2f is fitted into two binding energies at 532.5 and 534.7 eV, which correspond to CO and C–O, respectively. The C 1s spectrum of the CDs in Fig. 2g is divided into three peaks at 285.2, 287.7, and 288.8 eV, which were assigned to C–C/CC, CO/CN, and C–O, respectively. The deconvoluted N 1s XPS spectrum of CDs displays three peaks at 389.4, 402.0, and 402.4 eV, corresponding to amino N (C–N), pyridinic N (C–NC), and graphitic N (N–C3), respectively (Fig. 2h). The two characteristic peaks at 531.9 and 535 eV in the O 1s spectrum can be assigned to the binding energies of CO and C–O from CDs, as illustrated in Fig. 2i. Compared to CuCl-CDs, the C1s spectrum of CDs displayed more CC but a lower proportion of CO, revealing that pristine CDs possess a more sp2 hybridized structure to scavenge free radicals. Furthermore, pristine CDs have more surface amino N compared to CuCl-CDs, indicating that CDs show higher antioxidant characteristics due to the presence of N–H with low BDE values. The characteristic peak of CO in CuCl-CDs relative to pristine CDs was greatly enhanced, suggesting that CuCl-CDs can perform more efficient ROS generation. This is in accordance with a report by Qu et al., who showed that ketonic carbonyl groups (CO) in CDs were not only closely related to the ROS generation ability of CDs but also are the catalytically active centers that decompose H2O2 and generate •OH radicals [63,64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS scavenging/generation activities of CDs and CuCl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For a deeper understanding of the effect of heteroatom incorporation on the ROS regulation capabilities of CDs, radical scavenging and generating assays were used to evaluate their antioxidant and pro-oxidant activities. To examine the radical-scavenging activity, •O2 − and •OH radicals were chosen as model radicals using NBT and TMB enzymatic assays, respectively. In NBT assays, •O2 − radicals are produced by the reaction of the xanthine/xanthine oxidase system, which triggers the reduction of NBT to form formazan (purple color). The TMB chromogenic reaction was applied to detect the production of •OH radicals generated by the Fenton reaction (Fe2+ + H2O2 → Fe3+ + •OH + OH−). As displayed in Fig. 3 a and d, concentration-dependent elimination of •OH and •O2 − radicals of CDs was observed, suggesting that pristine CDs possess ROS scavenging characteristics. In Fig. 3c and f, the half-maximal inhibitory concentrations (IC50) of •O2 − and •OH radicals for pristine CDs were estimated 6.89 be 6.12 μg/mL, respectively.

After heteroatom incorporation, the as-prepared CuCl-CDs exhibited lower antioxidant activity than the pristine CDs under the same conditions. For example, the characteristic absorbance band of NBT formazan gradually decreased as the CuCl-CD dose was increased, as shown in Fig. 3b, resulting in lower inhibition activity of •O2 − than those treated with pristine CDs at all concentrations (Fig. 3a). Meanwhile, compared with the absorption spectra of the CDs and TMB solutions (Fig. 3d), it can be seen in Fig. 3e that CuCl-CDs show a gradual increase in the absorbance of oxTMB at 652 nm with increasing CuCl-CD concentrations. Importantly, as observed in Fig. 3f, the •OH inhibition rate of CuCl-CDs reduces with increasing CuCl-CD concentration, suggesting that CuCl-CDs show no •OH inhibition ability. In contrast, CuCl-CDs possess •OH generation ability, thereby catalyzing the oxidation of colorless TMB to blue oxTMB, leading to a higher absorbance of oxTMB at 650 nm. Based on these results, we hypothesized that CuCl-CDs have peroxidase-like activity to produce •OH radicals. To verify this hypothesis, the TMB assay was adopted to study the peroxidase-like activity of CuCl-CDs under different conditions. As shown in Fig. 3g, the mixture of TMB, CuCl-CDs, and H2O2 showed a characteristic absorbance at 652 nm (red line), suggesting that CuCl-CDs possess peroxidase-mimicking ability to catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into •OH at pH 5.5. As a control, TMB and CuCl-CDs exhibited negligible absorbance (brown line) in the absence of H2O2. However, the characteristic peak of oxTMB at 652 nm (green line) was observed when the TMB and CuCl-CD solutions were exposed to the laser for 10 min. This result indicated that CuCl-CDs exhibited a photodynamic effect to generate •OH radicals, which convert colorless TMB to blue oxTMB under laser irradiation. Moreover, Fig. 3g shows that the combination of CuCl-CDs, H2O2, and laser irradiation exhibited the highest characteristic peak of oxidized TMB (blue line), revealing that CuCl-CDs yielded higher •OH amounts due to the cooperative effect of photoexcitation and peroxidase-like activity.

To further assess the ROS generation capability of CuCl-CDs upon laser irradiation, DPBF and NBT were utilized as chemical probes for the detection of 1O2 and •O2 −, respectively. It is well known that DPBF readily undergoes a 1,4-cycloaddition reaction with 1O2 and subsequently decomposes to colorless 1,2-dibenzoylbenzene (Fig. S2a). Additionally, NBT was specifically reduced by •O2 − to produce dark blue NBT-formazan, as shown in Fig. S2b. From Fig. 3h, it is clear that the characteristic peak intensity of NBT-formazan at 260 nm increased with irradiation time, suggesting •O2 − formation when CuCl-CDs were irradiated by the laser. The results in Fig. 3i show that the fluorescence intensity of DPBF gradually decreased with increasing irradiation time, revealing that CuCl-CDs exhibited 1O2 generation ability under laser irradiation. On the other hand, ESR spectroscopy with DMPO and 4-oxo-TEMP as radical trapping agents was adopted to verify ROS species. DMPO can capture •OH radicals to form the DMPO-OH adduct, while 4-oxo-TEMP was employed to trap 1O2 to afford the stable nitroxide radical 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-oxo-pi-peridin-1-oxyl (4-oxo-TEMPO). Weak signals were observed for the CuCl-CDs in Fig. 4 a, while higher triplet signals with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1 were detected for CuCl-CDs upon laser irradiation. Fig. 4b also shows the 1O2 emission peak at 1270 nm, which corresponds to the electronic transition from the first excited singlet state of oxygen (1Δg) to the ground triplet state (3Σg –), generated by laser irradiation of CuCl-CDs in oxygen-saturated water. In Fig. 4c, the ESR spectrum of CuCl-CDs treated with H2O2 shows four-line peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1, which can be assigned to the DMPO–OH adduct.

When CuCl-CDs were simultaneously treated with H2O2 and laser irradiation, the ESR signals of DMPO–OH became stronger due to the generation of more •OH radicals, which is consistent with the measurements of the TMB assay (blue line in Fig. 3g). Because of the interference of •OH radicals and the lower reactivity of •O2 − towards DMPO which was dissolved in aqueous solution, DMSO solvent was used to replace water for the detection of DMPO-•O2 −. As shown in Fig. 4d, a quadruplet signal characteristic with equal intensities was observed, suggesting the formation of •O2 − when CuCl-CDs were irradiated by the laser. Overall, •O2 −, •OH, and 1O2 radicals were simultaneously produced in the CuCl-CDs upon laser irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of GCP nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: To further enhance the ROS-based treatment efficiency of CuCl-CDs, GCP nanocomposites consisting of CuCl-CDs, PDA, and GOx were developed and their utility in combinational PTT/PDT/CDT/starvation therapy of cancer cells was evaluated, as shown in Scheme 2 . A schematic illustration of the synthesis of the GCP nanocomposites is shown in Scheme 1b. PDA NPs were first synthesized through self-oxidation and polymerization of dopamine in a basic ammonia solution [65,66]. and their surface was further modified with PEG-NH2 (denoted as PDA-PEG) through Schiff base formation between quinones of PDA and amine of PEG-NH2, to improve the water solubility of the resulting compounds. Finally, CuCl-CDs and GOx were anchored onto the PDA-PEG NP surface through covalent interactions between the amines of CuCl-CDs [50,51] or amines/thiols of GOx [51,67,68] with quinones of PDA-PEG NPs, to obtain the GCP nanocomposites. FTIR spectroscopy was performed to investigate the surface modification of the GCP nanocomposites. Fig. 5 a shows the FTIR spectrum of PDA NPs, with absorption bands at 3580, 3340, 2932, 1689, 1323, and 1265 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching frequencies of O–H, N–H, C–H, CC vibration, N–H, and C–O bending, respectively. The acquired result agrees well with the findings of earlier reports [49,68]. Following PEG-NH2 modification, a new strong peak at 1121 cm−1 was found in the PDA-PEG spectra, which corresponds to the asymmetric C–O–C stretching frequency, indicating PEGylation of PDA. N–H bending, CC vibration, CN/CO, and C–H stretching were assigned to the peaks at 1409, 1499, 1684, and 2910 cm−1, respectively, while the broad signal around 3250–3600 cm−1 was ascribed to the presence of catechol groups. CuCl-CDs@PDA-PEG had a wider characteristic peak at 3299 cm−1 (N–H stretching) than bare PDA-PEG, indicating that the oxidized catechol group of PDAs was covalently connected to the amine group of CuCl-CDs. In addition, the peak at 1689 cm−1 corresponds to CN stretching, indicating that imine bond production is preferred. Similarly, because of the stretching frequency of CN, the absorption band of the GCP nanocomposites revealed prominent peaks at 1692 cm–1, confirming imine linkage formation. The O–H/N–H bonding (3200-3600 cm−1) was observed to be narrow, indicating that the catechol group of PDAs interacted with the nucleophilic amine group of the GOx enzyme. According to these findings, CuCl-CDs and GOx were covalently loaded onto PDA-PEG, resulting in GCP nanocomposites.

Fig. 5b and c shows TEM images of the PDA and PDA-PEG NPs with nearly spherical shapes. After the modification of CuCl-CDs and GOx, the as-synthesized GCP nanocomposites were quasi-spherical NPs surrounded by a grey shell, indicating the existence of CuCl-CDs and GOx (Fig. 5d). Moreover, Fig. S3a illustrates High-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) image of GCP nanocomposites. The existence of C, N, O, Cu, and Cl elements in GCP nanocomposites were further confirmed by EDS mapping shown in Figs. S3(b–f), respectively. The findings confirming that CuCl-CDs and GOx were coupled onto PDA-PEG NPs, resulting in a GCP nanocomposite. The average hydrodynamic diameters of PDA NPs, and GCP nanocomposites obtained by DLS are around 129, 133, and 135.5 nm, respectively (Fig. 5e–g). Zeta potential measurements were carried out to examine the charge distribution on the surface of the GCP nanocomposites. As shown in Fig. 5h, the CuCl-CDs showed a positive charge (4.71 mV). PDA NPs had negative zeta potential (−3.38 mV), which was changed to −2.5 mV after modification of PEG-NH2, indicating the formation of PDA-PEG NPs. The final GCP nanocomposites exhibited a positive surface charge (2.5 mV) after GOx and CuCl-CDs conjugated with PDA-PEG NPs. Notably, as demonstrated in Fig. S4a, GCP nanocomposites have long-term dispersibility in a variety of biological media, including water, PBS, FBS, and DMEM. The size of the nanocomposites was monitored every five days, and the DLS result (as shown in Fig. S4b) did not show a significant variation even after 15 days, indicating that GCP nanocomposites were more stable in the dispersion medium. The covalently linked PEG-NH2 between the PDA nanoparticles causes a steric hindrance effect that inhibits the nanoparticles from adhering or coagulating while preserving the nanocomposite's stability in the dispersion solvent. In addition, Fig. S4b shows the polydispersibility index (PDI) value of GCP nanocomposite, suggesting that the PDI value ranges from 0.23 to 0.35, demonstrating that GCP nanocomposites have better colloidal stability in various solvents as compared to the current reports [69,70]. Figs. S5a and S5b summarize the optical properties of CuCl-CDs, PDA-PEG NPs, and GCP nanocomposites. As shown in Fig. S5a, the PDA-PEG NPs had broad absorbance over a wide wavelength range with a long absorption tail at 800 nm. After CuCl-CD conjugation, the GCP nanocomposites not only show broad absorbance over a wide wavelength range with a long absorption tail to 800 nm but also have two shoulder peaks at 302 nm and 494 nm corresponding to π-π* and n-π* transitions of CuCl-CDs. As shown in Fig. S5b, the PDA-PEG NPs did not show any emission after excitation at 490 nm; however, the GCP nanocomposites exhibited emission at 628 nm, similar to CuCl-CDs.

The photothermal performance of the as-synthesized nanocomposites was explored by irradiating the GCP nanocomposite concentration gradient in aqueous solutions (50, 75, and 100 ppm) with 808 nm laser power (2.0 W/cm2) for 10 min. Fig. S5c shows that the temperature variation curve of the 100 ppm GCP nanocomposite solution rapidly increased from 24 °C to 49.8 °C after 10 min of laser irradiation, whereas the temperature difference of the control sample (water) was only increased by 3.5 °C under the same conditions, verifying that the GCP nanomaterial could efficiently convert NIR light to thermal energy. Furthermore, the GCP nanocomposites exhibited concentration-dependent and laser-power-dependent photothermal performance, as illustrated in Figs. S5c and S5d, respectively. The light-to-heat conversion efficiency (η) of the GCP nanocomposites was examined using Ropers’ method as shown below:

Based on the above equation, the change in temperature was given as ΔTmax (26.3 °C); Qdis refers to heat dissipation by light (13.02 mW); hA was calculated using

, I is the incident laser power (584.3 mW/cm2), and Aλ represents the absorbance of the sample (0.53) at 808 nm. The value for the heat exchange constant (τs = 489.1 s), as shown in Fig. S5e, according to the obtained data, η was calculated to be 24.4%, verifying that the GCP nanocomposites possess improved performance compared with a recent report [70,71] and could be considered as a promising candidate for photothermal agents. The photothermal stability of the GCP nanocomposites was also investigated by performing five successive heating/cooling cycles, illustrating that no obvious temperature decrease was observed (Fig. S5f). These results indicate the excellent thermal stability of the GCP nanocomposites with respect to photothermal conversion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enzymatic cascade activity of GCP nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: We next assessed the ability of GCP nanocomposites to catalyze the conversion of glucose into gluconic acid and H2O2 during glycolysis. The H2O2 concentration was quantified by the production of H2TiO4, which has a characteristic absorption peak at 405 nm, based on the reaction of titanium sulfate (Ti(SO4)2) and H2O2 (Ti4+ + H2O2 + 2H2O → H2TiO4 + 4H+). As displayed in Fig. 6 a, monitoring the absorption intensities of H2TiO4 at fixed wavelengths (410 nm) showed that GCP nanocomposites and free GOx both introduce relative absorption intensity of H2TiO4 increase with increasing glucose concentration, indicating H2O2 formation. Likewise, the other downstream product, gluconic acid, was analyzed by the subsequent formation of the hydroxamate complex. Briefly, gluconic acid can selectively react with hydroxylamine and produce an acid-hydroxamate complex, followed by combining with ferric ions to form a red solution with an absorbance peak at 505 nm (gluconic acid + hydroxylamine + Fe3+ → Hydroxamate-Fe3+). As shown in Fig. 6b, the relative absorption intensities of hydroxamate-Fe3+ at 505 nm increased with the elevated concentrations of glucose for GCP nanocomposites and free GOx, indicating that the GCP nanocomposite can catalyze the oxidation of glucose to generate gluconic acid.

Additionally, it can be seen that a significant pH change is a result of the formation of gluconic acid, for GCP nanocomposites or free GOx after treatment with glucose, as shown in Fig. 6c. In comparison, CuCl-CDs@PDA-PEG showed no obvious H2O2 consumption (Fig. 6a), no significant gluconic acid formation (Fig. 6b), and negligible pH variation (Fig. 6c) due to the absence of GOx modification. These results indicate that the GCP nanocomposites could mediate glucose consumption. Subsequently, the DPBF reagent acts as a detection probe to monitor ROS based on the chemical reaction shown in Fig. S2a. As depicted in Fig. 6d, the PL intensity of DPBF gradually decreased with increasing irradiation time, suggesting the photodynamic activity of the GCP nanocomposites under laser irradiation. The results in Fig. 6e show the relative PL intensity of DPBF treated with PDA-PEG or GCP under different conditions. The relative PL intensity of DPBF decreased in the following order: GCP + H2O2 + laser > GCP + laser > GCP + H2O2 > GCP alone. Furthermore, the relative PL intensity of DPBF treated with GCP, H2O2, and laser reduced with increasing irradiation time. Control experiments performed with PDA-PEG showed that the combination of laser and H2O2 or laser alone cannot affect the relative intensity variation of DPBF. These results were found to be consistent with the TMB assay in Fig. 3g and clearly support that CuCl-CDs endow GCP nanocomposites with photodynamic ability and peroxidase-mimic activity, which gave a high yield of ROS via the combination of H2O2 and laser irradiation.

The GCP nanocomposites possess promising peroxidase activity and convert H2O2 to •OH radicals due to the conjugation of CuCl-CDs. The peroxidase activities of the GCP nanocomposites were confirmed using the TMB colorimetric assay, a commonly used assay for the detection of •OH radicals. The UV–Vis spectroscopy measurements in Fig. 6f show the effects of pH on the peroxidase-mimicking activity of the GCP nanocomposites. It was shown that the characteristic peak of TMB + GCP + H2O2 in an acidic environment (pH 5.5) was much higher than that of reaction systems in a neutral environment (pH 7.5). We also evaluated the peroxidase-mimicking activity of the GCP nanocomposites without H2O2. The results in Fig. 6f show that no noticeable signal was observed for TMB + GCP in the absence of H2O2. As illustrated in Fig. 6g, the absorbance intensity of oxTMB at 652 nm increased the H2O2 amount for the reaction systems at pH 5.5. It was also found that the absorbance intensity enhancement of oxTMB was GCP concentration-dependent (Fig. 6h). All the experimental results revealed that GCP nanocomposites could convert H2O2 to •OH radicals and thereby oxidize TMB, suggesting that GCP nanocomposites exhibit peroxidase-mimic activities. To verify the catalytic rate, kinetic assays were employed to quantify the peroxidase activity of the GCP nanocomposites. The kinetic parameters Michaelis–Menten constant (Km) and maximal velocity (Vmax) were calculated using

and the output of the results were compared with horseradish peroxidase (HRP), a standard natural peroxidase enzyme. Figs. S6a and S6b show the typical Michaelis−Menten curves and Lineweaver-Burk model, respectively, within the appropriate range of H2O2, showing an increasing absorbance for oxTMB due to the rapid generation of •OH radicals by the GCP nanocomposites. Moreover, Km and Vmax values for H2O2 were determined as 24.45 × 10−3 M and 2.61 × 10−7 M S−1, respectively, indicating that GCP owned higher rate of reaction toward H2O2 decomposition. In brief, the Km value of GCP towards the H2O2 substrate is lower than that towards the HRP enzyme (Km ≈ 0.43 mM) [72,73]. For the glucose substrate, Michaelis−Menten curves and the Lineweaver-Burk model are presented in Figs. S6c and S6d, respectively, showing Km and Vmax values of approximately 18.10 × 10−3 M and 2.29 × 10−7 M S−1, respectively.

To assess the self-organized enzymatic cascade activity of the GCP nanocomposite, we designed a TMB colorimetric method without H2O2 for the detection of •OH radicals. First, the GCP nanocomposite catalyzed the oxidation of glucose to generate H2O2. Subsequently, the newly generated H2O2 could be catalyzed to produce •OH radicals, and thereby oxidize TMB with the appearance of a blue color. As depicted in Fig. 6i, the absorption value of the TMB solution at 652 nm gradually increased as the glucose concentration ranged from 10 to 100 μL. The results indicated that the GCP nanocomposite effectively depleted glucose and converted to •OH radicals without the addition of H2O2, which would be beneficial for tumor starvation therapy and CDT via glucose consumption and the generation of •OH radicals, respectively, as displayed in Scheme 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro evaluation of GCP nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Cytocompatibility experiments were carried out to investigate the biocompatibility of the as-prepared GCP nanocomposites using the WST-1 assay before proceeding to in vitro therapeutic studies. B16F1, HepG2, and HeLa cancer cells were incubated with GCP nanocomposites at different concentrations (100, 200, 300, 500, 700, and 1000 ppm) for 24, 48, and 72 h, respectively.

Fig. 7 a–c demonstrate that the GCP nanocomposites were compatible with B16F1, HepG2, and HeLa cancer cells within the given range of concentrations because cellular viabilities were greater than 85% after 72 h of incubation. The results confirmed that the GCP nanocomposites showed good biocompatibility and were a potential therapeutic candidate for long-term biological applications. To demonstrate that GCP nanocomposites can serve as phototherapeutic agents for tumor-cell killing, B16F1, HeLa, and HepG2 cells were used as cell models with single or two laser sources by using the WST-1 assay, and the results are shown in Fig. 7d–f. When the three types of cancer cells were incubated with various concentrations of GCP nanocomposites without laser irradiation, no noticeable cell death was observed. Meanwhile, there was no obvious cell toxicity after 808 nm or 532 nm laser irradiation alone. However, the survival rates of the three types of cancer cells decreased significantly after exposure to the combination of laser irradiation and GCP nanocomposites. For example, upon irradiation with 808 nm laser, the survival rates of B16F1, HeLa, and HepG2 cells were 34.5%, 65.8%, and 21.3%, respectively, at 200 ppm GCP nanocomposites due to the PTT effect, while upon irradiation with a 532 nm laser, the survival rates were 40.7%, 68.7%, and 30.7%, respectively, at 200 ppm GCP nanocomposites due to the PDT effect. Additionally, the utilization of two laser sources (808 and 532 nm) to induce simultaneous photodynamic and photothermal effects on B16F1, HeLa, and HepG2 cancer cells was used to evaluate the synergistic effect. As shown in Fig. 7d–f, only 20.7%, 59.7%, and 15.7% remained alive for B16F1, HeLa, and HepG2 cells, respectively, after treatment with 200 ppm GCP nanocomposites. The results indicated that the combined PDT/PTT provided the most efficient phototherapy for suppressing the growth of cancer cells.

To assess the cellular uptake and intracellular localization of GCP nanocomposites, B16F1 were chosen as model cells using confocal laser scanning microscopy. Fig. 7h shows confocal microscopic images of B16F1 cancer cells stained with DAPI and treated with GCP nanocomposites at different concentrations. The images clearly show that the blue fluorescence from DAPI is the location of cell nuclei, while the intense red fluorescence signal of GCP nanocomposites was dispersed throughout the cytoplasm and nucleus of B16F1 cells. In addition, more intracellular fluorescence intensities were observed with increasing GCP nanocomposite doses. The mean fluorescence intensity of a single cell calculated from Fig. S7 also exhibited a linear correlation between the fluorescence intensities and the concentration of GCP nanocomposites. According to the above results, the cellular uptake efficiency of GCP nanocomposites is dose-dependent, which supports that the in vitro cell killing efficacy of PDT and PTT is dependent on the concentration of GCP nanocomposites (Fig. 7d–f).

Additionally, ROS levels inside B16F1 cells were evaluated using DCFH-DA as the detection probe. DCFH-DA is a non-fluorescent molecule that can be converted to fluorescent DCF upon reaction with various ROS, such as 1O2 and •OH (Fig. 8 a). In a control experiment, B16F1 cells exposed to laser irradiation but without the treatment of GCP nanocomposites showed negligible green fluorescence owing to the absence of intracellular ROS production (Fig. 8b, panel I). As illustrated in panel II of Fig. 8b, the weak green fluorescence signal originating from DCF was recorded when B16F1 cells were cultured with 75 ppm GCP nanocomposites and irradiated with a 532 nm laser. The confocal images in panel IV of Fig. 8b also reveal that the green fluorescence intensity increased with the elevated concentrations of GCP nanocomposites from 75 ppm to 200 ppm. To further evaluate ROS generation via the enzymatic cascade activity of GCP nanocomposites, B16F1 cells were incubated with both glucose and GCP nanocomposites in the dark. As shown in panel V of Fig. 8b, green fluorescence intensity could be observed because glucose could be catalyzed to generate •OH radicals through cascade catalytic reactions of GCP nanocomposites. Furthermore, as displayed in panel VI of Fig. 8b, stronger intracellular fluorescence of DCF was observed in B16F1 cells with the combination of light irradiation, glucose, and GCP nanocomposites. These results suggested that GCP nanocomposites could generate more intracellular ROS due to synergistic photodynamic effects and enzymatic cascade activity, as shown in Scheme 2.

Next, we evaluated the glucose-mediated starvation effect on the viability of B16F1 cells incubated in a culture medium containing a series of GCP nanocomposite concentrations. As shown in Fig. 8c, GCP nanocomposites induced cell toxicity under 100 or 200 ppm glucose and exhibited increased cell inhibition along with an increase in concentration. These results suggest that the GCP nanocomposite could generate toxic ROS to inhibit cell proliferation based on its enzymatic cascade activity. In other words, the GCP nanocomposite has the potential to consume glucose to starve cancer cells in the tumor region. Moreover, it was found that the enhancement of cell growth occurred in the absence of GCP nanocomposites for 100 and 200 ppm glucose. This may be attributed to the fact that glucose is a crucial nutrient supplier that facilitates cell proliferation. This trend is in agreement with previous report [74]. suggesting that the cell viability of 4T1 cells was more than 150% when using 200 or 400 μg/mL glucose for the starvation test. Subsequently, the combination of glucose-mediated starvation therapy, PTT, and PDT on the cell killing effect was investigated by applying the lasers to cells incubated with glucose and GCP nanocomposites. As shown in Fig. 8d, the cytotoxicity of the GCP nanocomposites showed a dose-dependent increase with increasing nanocomposite concentration under glucose treatment and laser irradiation. The results revealed that either the glucose +532 nm laser or the glucose +808 nm laser exhibited a higher cell killing effect than glucose alone. Particularly, the combination of glucose and 808/532 nm laser achieved better growth inhibition of cancer cells than the combination of glucose +808 laser (or 532 nm laser). Taken together, these results demonstrate that GCP nanocomposites provide more effective cancer ablation than single therapy based on their cooperative effect of starvation therapy and phototherapy.

In summary, we prepared CDs and CuCl-CDs exhibiting ROS scavenging and generation activities, respectively, via microwave-assisted pyrolysis. NBT and TMB enzymatic assays revealed that CDs are effective radical scavengers that perform concentration-dependent elimination of •OH and •O2 − radicals. Moreover, through Cu and Cl incorporating in the framework of CDs, it is possible to reverse the antioxidant properties of pristine CDs to cytotoxic pro-oxidants, which can efficiently produce •O2 −, •OH, and 1O2 radicals under light exposure. The developed strategy in the ROS generation/scavenging modulation of CDs may shed light on the development of CD-based nanomedicine for various therapeutic applications including bacterial infection, wound healing, inflammatory diseases. Additionally, CuCl-CDs were further integrated with PDA and GOx to form a multifunctional GCP nanocomposite. As a result, GCP nanocomposites not only efficiently converted laser light into thermal energy and cytotoxic ROS, but also mediated the glucose consumption process and converted to •OH radicals based on the enzymatic cascade activity. Overall, this study reveals the potential of GCP nanocomposites to act as phototherapeutic and starvation-inducing agents for cooperative cancer therapy via systematic in vitro investigations at the intracellular level. We anticipate that GCP nanocomposites might serve as a potential therapeutic candidate in future clinical applications.
